query: effect of BRAFV600E mutation on clinical response to radioiodine therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Radioactive Iodine Therapy for Thyroid Malignancies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Molecular Testing and Precision Medicine in Thyroid Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: The integration of molecular testing into managing differentiated thyroid cancer has gained prominence in recent years. Identifying genetic mutations, such as those involving BRAFV600E and RAS , has been valuable in making treatment decisions, including whether patients should receive radioactive iodine therapy. For example, patients with BRAFV600E mutations are more likely to benefit from radioactive iodine therapy due to the mutation's association with more aggressive disease and higher recurrence rates. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Vemurafenib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Vemurafenib is a selective BRAF inhibitor approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating metastatic and unresectable melanoma with a BRAF V600 mutation, as well as Erdheim-Chester disease. This drug works by selectively inhibiting the mutated BRAF V600E kinase, thereby blocking MAPK pathway signaling and reducing tumor cell proliferation and growth. Additionally, vemurafenib is used off-label for other BRAF-mutant cancers, including refractory non-small cell lung carcinoma and papillary thyroid cancer, as well as metastatic or unresectable melanoma with the BRAF V600K mutation. Clinical trials (BRIM I–III) demonstrated improved survival in BRAF-mutant melanoma, leading to FDA approval. Common adverse effects include skin reactions and photosensitivity, especially with radiation therapy. Due to potential toxicity, regular monitoring of liver, kidney, and cardiac function is essential. Interprofessional collaboration is key to optimizing treatment and minimizing adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Cytotoxic chemotherapy alone is of limited benefit. Taxanes, doxorubicin, and cisplatin have been used with variable success. Paclitaxel, doxorubicin, and carboplatin are often used as radiosensitizing agents in administering concurrent chemoradiation. Identifying molecular targets has heralded a new therapy era, with treatments targeting specific mutations. [23] These include the following molecular targets and the respective agents: BRAF/V600E mutations : Dabrafenib/trametenib NTRK gene fusions : Larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib RET gene fusions : Pralsetinib, selpercatinib

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Radioactive iodine-refractory DTC: 24 mg orally daily

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: In patients diagnosed with anaplastic thyroid cancer, BRAF V600E mutation testing and staging are performed. Resectable disease is surgically removed, followed by specific BRAF kinase inhibitors in patients with BRAF V600E mutations. Other patients received targeted radiation treatment and cytotoxic chemotherapy after surgery. However, distant metastases are common in patients even at initial diagnosis due to their rapidly progressive course, and local invasion into the trachea or vasculature may occur, making it unresectable. Mortality is near 100% for these cancers, and a conservative surgical approach for palliation can be considered in such high-risk patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Oncocytic (Hürthle Cell) Thyroid Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with metastatic oncocytic carcinoma who are asymptomatic and have well-differentiated tumors with slow-growing disease, observation is an acceptable option as long as they do not have brain metastases. However, for patients with symptoms or rapidly growing metastatic tumors that respond to iodine treatment, RAI is a preferable option. If the patient's tumor is not suitable for RAI, systemic therapy with tyrosine kinase inhibitors (eg, lenvatinib or sorafenib) is considered. [13] [14] Lenvatinib is preferred over sorafenib due to its better adverse effect profile. However, because oncocytic cell tumors are rare, most of the data for using tyrosine kinase inhibitors in metastatic disease is extrapolated from other thyroid carcinomas. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: This is reserved for patients with recurrent or excessive remnant disease after the initial surgical treatment and radioactive iodine ablation. The most common agents used are the kinase inhibitors that target different pathways, like the anti-angiogenic multi-targeted kinase inhibitors (aaMKI- lenvatinib, sorafenib), BRAF kinase inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib), MEK inhibitors (trametinib, cobimetinib), NTR kinase inhibitors (larotrectinib), and RET inhibitors (selpercatinib). [37] [38] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Radioactive Iodine Therapy for Thyroid Malignancies -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Each major type of thyroid carcinoma has a distinct pathogenesis. Malignancies derived from thyroid follicular epithelium generally harbor driver mutations that activate protein kinase pathways, promoting carcinogenesis. Most papillary thyroid carcinomas develop after activating the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling pathway. Follicular thyroid carcinomas frequently exhibit mutations in RAS or components of the phosphatidylinositol-3 kinase (PI3K)/AKT signaling pathways. [11] Progression from well-differentiated to poorly differentiated thyroid cancer typically involves additional mutations that further alter cellular function.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Molecular Testing
retrieved_docs.content: BRAF status, microsatellite instability, NTRK, RET, ALK mutations, and tumor mutational burden should be assessed. In particular, BRAF mutations should be evaluated, as these mutations can change the determined treatment approaches. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosomal rearrangements have been identified in PTC, including the RET protooncogene, NTRK1, and MET gene overexpression. [11] Further, mutations in the BRAF gene have also been identified and correlated with prognosis, particularly BRAFV600E mutation. [12] [13] [14] [15] A clear association between PTC and radiation exposure is now well established.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic chemotherapy for DTC is preferably administered through a clinical trial. The common agents of choice are the kinase inhibitor class of drugs such as anti-angiogenic multi-targeted kinase inhibitors (aaMKI- lenvatinib, sorafenib), BRAF kinase inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib), MEK inhibitors (trametinib, cobimetinib), NTR kinase inhibitors (larotrectinib), and RET inhibitor (selpercatinib). [56] The choice of agent depends on the occurrence of specific gene mutations or signaling irregularities such as those described above. For patients with no identifiable mutations, aaMKIs are the recommended first-line therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A retrospective study evaluated the efficacy of lenvatinib in patients with unresectable radioactive iodine therapy refractory differentiated thyroid cancer across multiple institutions. Fifty-one patients were analyzed, and overall survival and progression-free survival were calculated. Planned drug holidays were implemented to mitigate adverse events. The study found that patients with planned drug holidays experienced significantly improved overall survival, progression-free survival, and time to treatment failure compared to those without planned drug holidays. However, various administration schedules were observed during planned drug holidays. The study underscores the importance of an individualized, patient-centered approach to administration schedules to optimize treatment outcomes in patients with DTC.

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lenvatinib was approved for use in radioactive iodine-refractory DTC following a randomized, multicenter, placebo-controlled phase III study (SELECT). The trial included 392 participants with radioactive iodine-refractory DTC and was subject to random assignment to either a treatment group of 24 mg lenvatinib daily or the placebo group at a 2:1 ratio. The treatment group demonstrated a median progression-free survival of 18.3 months and a response rate of 64.8% compared to the placebo group, 3.6 months and 1.5%, respectively. Both groups' overall survival did not show a notable difference, which may have been from the crossover between the placebo and treatment groups. [1] Lenvatinib has also been studied for use in anaplastic thyroid cancer but is currently not FDA-approved for this indication. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Cancer -- Evaluation -- Role of Molecular Testing in Thyroid Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Molecular testing is generally used in Bethesda categories 3 and 4, where cytology is indeterminate. [34] Recently, testing for single mutations such as BRAF V600E or RET/PTC translocations was performed. While these tests yield good specificity (100%), their sensitivity is usually poor (50 to 60%). retrieved_docs.title: Vemurafenib -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to its FDA-approved indications, vemurafenib has been used off-label in the treatment of several other BRAF-mutated malignancies, including: Metastatic and unresectable melanoma with BRAF V600K mutation [16] Refractory non-small cell lung carcinoma BRAF V600 mutation [17] BRAF V600E-positive refractory metastatic or unresectable papillary thyroid cancer [18] Refractory hairy cell leukemia [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: Other diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals for tumour detection in ATC and Technetium 99m compounds, tumor detection radiopharmaceuticals are both classified as tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals. These agents are used for diagnostic imaging of neoplasms, with the former referring to additional radiopharmaceuticals employed specifically for detecting tumours in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma, and the latter denoting Technetium‑99m–based compounds that serve the same diagnostic purpose. Recognizing both categories under the broader class of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals highlights the variety of radioactive agents available for tumour imaging and supports appropriate selection in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 115
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride supplements are classified as fluorine compounds and are associated with fluoridy, indicating a relationship with fluoride ions. Fluorocholine is also a fluorine compound and serves as a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical specifically for tumour detection in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC); it belongs to the broader category of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals. Similarly, the class “Indium‑111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals” is a subset of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals and includes indium In‑111 pentetreotide as a member. Indium In‑111 pentetreotide functions by targeting somatostatin receptors, enabling imaging of tumours that express these receptors. Together, fluoride‑based agents and radiopharmaceuticals such as fluorocholine and indium In‑111 pentetreotide illustrate the use of fluorine‑containing compounds and radio‑labeled molecules in diagnostic imaging and therapeutic strategies for cancer detection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: within the ATC tumor‑detection group, underscoring the importance of fluorine chemistry in oncologic imaging. Technetium‑99m‑based tumor agents are further specialized. Technetium‑99m‑depreotide is derived from a somatostatin analogue, retaining affinity for somatostatin receptors and enhancing imaging of neuroendocrine and other somatostatin‑receptor‑positive tumors. Technetium‑99m aggregated albumin represents an additional child agent within the technetium‑99m family. Collectively, these entities form an interconnected network: radiofarmaka encompass diagnostic radioisotopes; diagnostic radioisotopes and fluorine‑based agents such as fluorocholine and fluoride supplements serve specific imaging or preventive functions; and technetium‑99m agents engineered from biologically active peptides (e.g., somatostatin) improve tumor detection. Understanding these hierarchical and functional relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate radiopharmaceuticals for accurate imaging,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1397
retrieved_docs.content: The GDC Therapeutic Agent Terminology encompasses a range of therapeutic agents, and within this classification the concept of Thyroid Extract is included as a distinct subset, indicating that Thyroid Extract is recognized as a specific therapeutic agent in the GDC terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 348
retrieved_docs.content: Indium 111In‑satumomab pendetide and indium In‑111 pentetreotide are both members of the class Indium‑111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals, which in turn is a specific subclass of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals. As a type of tumour detection radiopharmaceutical, Indium‑111 tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals are used to image malignant lesions by delivering the gamma‑emitting isotope indium‑111 to target tissues. Indium In‑111 pentetreotide is designed to bind to somatostatin receptors (receptory somatostatinu), allowing selective visualization of tumors that express these receptors. In addition, other diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals employed for tumour detection in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) are also categorized as tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals, underscoring a broader group of agents used for nuclear imaging of cancer. Together, these relationships clarify the hierarchical classification of indium‑111–based imaging agents and highlight the receptor‑targeted mechanism of indium In‑111 pentetreotide, which is relevant for precise oncologic diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2002
retrieved_docs.content: Chest imaging for metastatic carcinoma of thyroid is a diagnostic procedure that directly targets the chest (hrudník) and incorporates a radioisotope study of the endocrine system as a subordinate component. Closed drainage of chest is a therapeutic intervention performed within the chest region, which serves as an indirect procedural site and is associated with the subdivision of the thorax. Both the imaging study and the drainage procedure involve the chest area, linking diagnostic evaluation of metastatic thyroid cancer to therapeutic management of thoracic conditions such as pleural effusion, and highlighting the relevance of coordinated imaging and interventional techniques in patient care.

Fluorocholine, a fluorine‑based diagnostic radiopharmaceutical, belongs to a class used for tumor detection in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma; other radiopharmaceuticals for this indication are grouped with fluorocholine. Organometallic compounds connect to both mineral and diuretic categories: mercurial diuretics are members of the broader “miscellaneous diuretics” group and are linked to organometallic chemistry, as is calcium glubionate. These relationships illustrate how mineral‑based supplements, organometallic diuretics, calcium‑based replacement agents, and diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals are interrelated, supporting classification, prescribing, formulation, electrolyte management, dermatologic care, bleeding prophylaxis, and oncologic imaging decisions.

This imaging modality can serve as a parent procedure that includes more specific sub‑studies or child procedures under the umbrella of endocrine radioisotope evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
retrieved_docs.content: Technetium Tc 99m depreotide is a radiopharmaceutical that is mapped from the peptide hormone somatostatin, meaning it is a somatostatin analog labeled with technetium‑99m. By retaining the binding affinity of somatostatin for its receptors, technetium Tc 99m depreotide is used in nuclear medicine to image tissues that express somatostatin receptors, such as neuroendocrine tumors and other receptor‑positive lesions, thereby aiding diagnosis and patient management.
